coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive electrochemical detection of E. coli O157:H7 using conductive carbon dot/ZnO nanorod/PANI composite electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Pangajam, A.
Theyagarajan, K.
Dinakaran, K.
coredata.dc:description: A selective electrochemical sensor based on carbon dot (CD)/ZnO nanoroad/PANI nanoassembly has been developed towards the detection of cadmium (II) present in the environmental samples. In this work, a conductive nanocomposite electrode, CDs/ZnO/PANI, was successfully fabricated by polymerizing aniline (PANI) in the presence of carbon dot and Zinc oxide nanorod and followed by coating onto printed carbon sheet. These CDs were nearly spherical with good size distribution and excellent monodispersity, and the average size of CD were around 3–5 nm as evidence from Transmission electron microscope (TEM). ZnO nanorod having average length of 20 nm has been synthesized and characterized by TEM. The nanocomposite CDs/ZnO/PANI was characterized by Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical analyzer. SEM investigation confirms that the CD and ZnO are uniformly dispersed in PANI matrix. In addition, the outstanding electrical conductivity of CD/ZnO/PANI was found to increase the sensitivity for the detection of E.Coli. The developed electrochemical biosensor also showed good selectivity and was successfully detected E. coli O157:H7 with a detection limit of 1.3×10−18M, in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: E. coli sensor
DNA hybridization
PANI
Carbon dot
Electrochemical sensor
Differential pulse voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of the DNA biosensor
Stability of the DNA biosensor under successive CV scans for 50 cycles after incubating with 1.3 × 10−18 M target ssDNA

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Kerala

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledges the financial support of SERB, Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi, India , through Grant No. EEQ/2016/000049 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectra of CDs/PANI, ZnO/PANI, CDs/ZnO/PANI;
(a) TEM images of CDs–nanodots and (b) & (c) TEM images of ZnO – nanorod.
SEM images of (a) ZnO nanroads and (b)CDs/ZnO/PANI nanocomposites.
(a) CV curves and (b) Impedance spectra of polymer nanocomposites.
Analytical performance of the DNA biosensor (a)DPV curves of the modified electrode with various ssDNA concentrations, (b) the calibration plot of DPV peak current vs. concentration of ssDNA.
Validation of specificity of CDs/ZnO/PANI /pDNA modified electrodefor the detection of E. coli. i) Vibriocholerae, ii) E. coli, iii) Salmonella sp. iv) C. parvum.
Stability of the DNA biosensor under successive CV scans for 50 cycles after incubating with 1.3 × 10−18 M target DNA.
The principle of the developed label free electrochemical sensor based on CDs/ZnO/PANI for the detection of E. coli).
Comparison of different electrodes for the electrochemical determination of pathogens involving DNA hybridization.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive electrochemical detection of E. coli O157:H7 using conductive carbon dot/ZnO nanorod/PANI composite electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Thiruvalluvar University, Vellore 632 115, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Thiruvalluvar University, Vellore – 632 115, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Advanced Sciences, VIT University, Vellore 632 007, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Advanced Sciences, VIT University, Vellore – 632 007, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A selective electrochemical sensor based on carbon dot (CD)/ZnO nanoroad/PANI nanoassembly has been developed towards the detection of cadmium (II) present in the environmental samples. In this work, a conductive nanocomposite electrode, CDs/ZnO/PANI, was successfully fabricated by polymerizing aniline (PANI) in the presence of carbon dot and Zinc oxide nanorod and followed by coating onto printed carbon sheet. These CDs were nearly spherical with good size distribution and excellent monodispersity, and the average size of CD were around 3–5 nm as evidence from Transmission electron microscope (TEM). ZnO nanorod having average length of 20 nm has been synthesized and characterized by TEM. The nanocomposite CDs/ZnO/PANI was characterized by Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical analyzer. SEM investigation confirms that the CD and ZnO are uniformly dispersed in PANI matrix. In addition, the outstanding electrical conductivity of CD/ZnO/PANI was found to increase the sensitivity for the detection of E.Coli. The developed electrochemical biosensor also showed good selectivity and was successfully detected E. coli O157:H7 with a detection limit of 1.3×10−18M, in water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence pathogenic bacteria in food and water have become a global concern of food safety problems [1]. The control and prevention of foodborne pathogens rely on the rapid and accurate detection of foodborne pathogenic bacteria in food and water remains an important issue [2]. The analysis on the presence of E. coli in food and water is one of the most representative indicators [3–5] for the assessment of possible microorganism contamination. A number of methods have been developed to detect E. coli O157:H7 in food and water matrices, including traditional culturing with selective media [6,7], serotyping with specific antibodies to O157 and H7 antigens [8–11], amplification of specific genes by PCR [12–15], or hybridization of virulence genes by DNA microarrays [16]. In addition, various optical biosensors were also reported for the detection of pathogenic bacteria, using fluorescence or surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [17–21]. However, these methods have limitations with respect to the high cost, long time of assay, usage of highly toxic substances, Thus the need for the development of more eco-friendly, rapid and less laborious detection technology with high sensitivity and specificity to quantify small numbers of water-borne E. coli. Recently, biosensors based on DNA hybridization are widely studied in the detection of pathogen, cancer diagnosis, heavy metal detection due to its ultrasensitive, low cost, specificity and sensitivity [22–26]. In this regard, electrochemical sensors hold significant attention because of their reasonable limit of detection (LOD), reliability, affordability and ease of handling. Besides, combining the principles of immunological analysis in detecting analytes electrochemically, it provides the added advantage of high selectivity and specificity [27]. Electrochemical biosensing techniques usually offer highly-effective methods for the hybridization analysis of DNA [28–30], but DNA hybridization biosensors based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy is time consuming and may deteriorate the sensing performance. Difference pulse voltammetry (DPV) is one of preferred methods due to high sensitivity, extraordinary resolution, ultratrace analysis, and fast response. Antibodies and 1-pyrene butanoic acid succinimidyl ester modified single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) showed a significant enhancement in the resistance when the electrode was exposed to E. coli O157:H7 whole cells and the modified electrode showed a lowest detection limit of 105 and 103CFU (colony forming units)/mL [31]. Xu et al. reported a functional porous pseudo‑carbon paste electrode fabricated using carboxylic group containing multi-walled carbon nanotubes, glutaraldehyde and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane. The modified electrodes showed a significant electrochemical signal towards E. coli O157:H7 with a linear relationship between the concentration of E. coli O157:H7 and anodic stripping peak current from 1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 107 cell/mL and a limit of detection was reported to be as low as 8 × 102 cell/mL [32]. A new impedimetric sensing of Escherichia coli O157:H7 using antibody antigen binding method based on covalently linked antibody on a conducting Au/PANI/Glu sensor with the upper detection limit of 107CFU mL−1 [33].

In the present research, we have developed a nanocomposite polyanilne/carbon dot/zinc oxide nanorod electode based electrochemical biosensor for sensitive and specific detection of pathogenic E. coli O157:H7 bacteria. This biosensor was fabricated by the insitu polymerization of anline in the presesnce of carbon dot and zinc oxide nanorod. Polyaniline naocomposite was functionalized with O157:H7-specific amine functionalised oligo nucleotide that captured genomic DNA of E. coli O157:H7 on the sensor surface. Moreover, using conducting polymer such as PANI as a matrix for Zinc oxide nanorod and carbon dot dispersion are anticipated to enhance the sensitivity of electrochemical biosensors. PANI is a promising conducting polymer due to its excellent electrochemical properties, ease of synthesis, environmental stability and low toxicity [34–39]. Semiconductor metal oxide nanoparticles such as zinc oxide are also widely used in the development of electrochemical sensors because they facilitate the electron transfer process at the electrode–solution interface by improving the sensitivity of the modified electrodes. [40], due to its direct bandgap energy, large exciton binding energy [41–44]. In addition carbon nanodots contains honey is a natural product having 80% of carbon content. It is reported that the fluorescent carbon dot can be prepared from Honey by hydrothermal process at 120 °C. Hence, In the present work Conductive Carbon dot from natural honey is preferred having composed of sp2 carbons and a surface carrying carboxylic groups and hydroxyl groups, are water soluble, low toxic and are photoluminescent due to quantum confinement [45,46] Recently carbon dot has been employed in the form of core-shell nanoparticle having platinum as shell for electrochemical biosensing of DNA damage biomarker-8-hydroxy-20-deoxyguanosine [47].

The solution of oligonucleotide is prepared in Tris–EDTA buffer (1 M Tris–HCl, 0.5 M EDTA) of pH 8.0 and stored at −20 °C prior to use. Honey was bought from super market. 30% Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), Zinc acetate, absolute anhydrous ethanol (99.7%), aniline (99%), potassium per sulfate, were purchased from SRL, Fischer scientific and Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals and solvents were obtained from the commercial sources and used directly without further purification, and all glassware was cleaned successively with aqua regia and D.I water and then dried before use. Deionized water and milli Q water was used for all experiments. 0.1 mol/L acetate buffer solution (ABS, pH = 5.0) was prepared by mixing the appropriate amounts of sodium acetate and acetic acid. Double deionized water was prepared by Milli-Q water purification system. Nitrogen gas was used for purging oxygen in a solution to provide an inert atmosphere. Probe DNA (pDNA): amine-50-GGT CCG CTT GCT CTC GC-30 and the Target DNA (gDNA): genomic DNA are purchased from Synergy Scientific products (India).

Synthesis of carbon dots using the hydrogen peroxide and distilled water. It is a hydrothermal method. Briefly explain the procedure of carbon dots, naturally available honey 2 g was stirred for 10 min, then added 8 mL of distilled water to get the transparent solution and added 2 mL of 30% hydrogen peroxide. The content was transferred into Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 120 °C for 2 h and cooled to room temperature and this solution pH was adjusted to neutral with 2 M NaOH. The larger particles were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to obtain the Carbon Dots.

Polymerization of aniline was performed using CDs (without using any organic acid) via chemical oxidative polymerization technique. Briefly, at first 2 g of CDs were dispersed in water to get homogenous dispersion. Then, 200 mg of aniline was slowly added to the CDs and stirred continuously for one hour at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixture was cooled to 10 °C and the polymerization of aniline was initiated by using equimolar solution of potassium per sulfate as an oxidant. The polymerization was continued for 24 h at −5 °C temperature. After completion of the reaction, dark green colored precipitate was obtained. The precipitate was washed several times with water and methanol to remove the oligoanilines and excess potassium persulfate. Finally, the product was dried at room temperature in vaccum for 24 h to obtain CDs/PANI.

1 M solution consisting of 0.8 g NaOH and 20 mL C2H5OH and 0.084 M solution consisting of 0.918 g of Zn (CH3COO)2 and 60 mLC2H5OH were stirred by magnetic stirring at the 70 °C for 30 min, then the solution was cooled in ice-bath for 30 min. The precipitation ZnO from solution was carried out by addition of n-hexane. The ratio of volume of n-hexane to sol was 3:2. After the reaction completed, the resulted white products were centrifugalized and re-dispersed into ethanol. The removal of the ions possibly remaining in the final product from dispersed ZnO was accompolished by repetitive washing of ZnO with ethanol and n-hexane.

ZnO/PANI nanocomposite was synthesized by following steps: the 2 g of ZnO powder was added in aniline solution (1 mL aniline). Then 50 mL solution of 0.1 M potassium persulfate mixed with above solution with vigorous magnetic agitation at 5 °C for 2 h. A greenish black precipitate was obtained after keeping the resulting mixture at low temperature (5-10 °C) over night. The precipitate was filtered and washed several times with distilled water to remove unreacted chemicals. The precipitate was dried in an oven at 50 °C and then kept in desiccators to obtaineZnO/PANI Nanocomposites.

Polymerization of aniline was performed using CDs and ZnO (without using any organic acid) via chemical oxidative polymerization technique. Briefly, at first 2 g of CDs and 2 g of ZnO were dispersed in water to get homogenous dispersion. Then, 200 mg of aniline was slowly added to the CDs and stirred continuously for one hour at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixture was cooled to 10 °C and the polymerization of aniline was initiated by using equimolar solution of potassium persulfate as an oxidant. The polymerization was continued for 24 h at -5 °C temperature. After completion of the reaction, dark green colored precipitate was obtained. The precipitate was washed several times with water and methanol to remove the oligoanilines and excess potassium persulfate. Finally, the product was dried at room temperature in vaccum for 24 h to obtain CDs/ZnO/PANI Nanocomposites.

The ZnO/CDs/PANI (20 μL) was coated with SPCE. The modified electrode again was coated with 10 μL of 1.3 nM Aminated capture probe solution was added to the surface of the ZnO/CDs/PANI and incubated overnight at room temperature. Resulting the DNA modified electrode surface binding site of the electrode. Shown in Fig. 6 the stepwise process for constructing the DNA sensor, and the corresponding signal amplification of ZnO/CNDs/PANI based on DNA hybridization assays.

The electrode was incubated in the required concentration of target DNA solution for 2 h at room temperature, and 5 μL of the ZnO/CDs/PANI labelled probe solution was dropped onto the electrode surface and incubated 2 h. Electrochemical measurements were afterwards performed using a conventional three-electrode system. DPV measurements were taken between - 0.3 V and 0.4 V (vs. SCE) at a scan rate of 100 mV s in 0.1MPBS (pH 7.0).

Fig. 1 shows to the UV–Vis spectra of the prepared carbon dots and PANI functionalized carbon dots. It was ascertained that the absorption maximum of carbon dots is 281 nm which can be attributed to the existence of aromatic π orbital's (owing to n–π* transition) of carbon dots, and a absorption band at 366 nm is attributed to the π orbital's (owing to π –π* transition) of polyaniline, that the absorption maximum of Zinc Oxide nanorod show the two absorptionowing the 290 nm and 270 nm.

FTIR spectrum of the nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 2 . The band appeared at 3255 cm−1 corresponding to –NH- stretching frequency, the aromatic C–H stretching frequency appear at 3034 cm−1, the aromatic C–C stretching frequency appear at 1041 cm−1, absorption for C–O stretching frequency at 1528 cm−1 due to carbon dot and t Zn–O stretching frequency at 696 cm−1 it due to Zinc oxide nanorod.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of CDs-nanodots are shown in Fig. 3a, shows spherical and uniform size distribution carbon dot showing an average diameter of 3-5 nm. Fig. 3b–c shows the morphology of ZnO nonorod. The image clearly indicates the rod like which has an average length of 20 nm.

SEM images were taken to observe the morphological features of the nanomaterial and polymer nanocomposites. As illustrated in Fig. 4a shows the aggregates of ZnO nanorods and Fig. 4b shows a large lamellate and scrolled sheet of CDs/ZnO/PANI nanocomposites, providing a large specific surface area. Since the size of CDs are around 20 nm it forms homogeneous nanocomposite without any aggregation [48], unlike grapheme which reported to form aggregates on polymer matrix.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to investigate the characteristics of different modified electrodes (bare ITO, ZnO/PANI, CDs/PANI, CDs/ZnO/PANI and CDs/ZnO/PANI/p-DNA) CV was carried out in 1.0 mmol L−1 [Fe (CN)6]3−/4- (1:1) solution containing 0.1 molL−1KCl. As shown in Fig. 5 reversible redox peaks were obtained at the bare ITO. The relatively larger anodic and cathodic current was observed; after the ITO was modified with CDs/ZnO/PANI can be due to the large specific area and better conductivity. The CV response of CDs/ZnO/PANI modified ITO showed the largest current signal due to the cooperation of CDs/ZnO/PANI conductive composites. The reason isassigned to that the ZnO nanorod and CDs approaches the critical value which accounts for the increase in conductivity of composites.Another noteworthy cause for comparative enhancement ofelectrical conductivity is the polymer encapsulation in ZnO nanorod and CDs filler in the composites. On increasing the ZnO nanorod and CDs concentration, the neighbouring distance of particles in the polymer will get reduced, and subsequently, the electrical conductivity of CDs/ZnO/PANIis enhanced. The incorporation of ZnO nanorod and CDs is creditworthy for a charge distribution and synergy effect in the composites, which plays a significant role inenhancing the conductivity.EIS of polymer composites, has been studied to provide information about the interface properties and impedance changes in the process of electrode modification. It is observed from Fig. 5b, that the bare ITO has a relative large electron-transfer resistance (Rct) value of about 315 Ω.At, CDs/ZnO/PANI - ITO the value of resistance was changed to 161 Ω, suggesting that the bare ITO was coated efficiently by CDs/ZnO/PANI. A distinct decrease in the interfacial electron transfer resistance was also observed when PANI with superior electrical conductivity can accelerate the electron transfer on the electrode.

The amine terminated capture probe self-assembled CDs/ZnO/PANI modified electrode surface showed decreased the peak current, which suggests that the pDNA blocked the electron transfer.The redox peaks decreased further after the electrode was immersed into the target DNA solutionfor 2 h whichindicates that the oligonucleotides of the target ssDNA were successfully hybridized with the capture aptamer probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the DNA biosensor
Stability of the DNA biosensor under successive CV scans for 50 cycles after incubating with 1.3 × 10−18 M target ssDNA
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the DNA biosensor was observed in Fig. 8 , the stability of successive assays was studied over 50 cycles of CV measurements in PBS after incubation with 15 nM of E.Coli. The result suggests that the peak current has been decreased.

In summary, CD/ZnO/PANI conductive layers were prepared by in situ polymerization of aniline in teh presence of CD/ZnO and used as modified electrode for ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of E. coli O157:H7. Due to the enhanced conductivity of nanocomposite later, the sensitivity of the proposed sensor was twice when compared to that of the DNA based biosensor reported in the literature. In addition, the outstanding electrical conductivity of CD/ZnO/PANI was found to increase the sensitivity for the detection of E.Coli. The developed electrochemical biosensor also showed good selectivity and was successfully used to detect E. coli O157:H7 in water samples with a detection limit of 1.3 ×10−18M, indicating a promising method for sensitive electrochemical detection of pathogens.
The authors acknowledges the financial support of SERB, Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi, India, through Grant No. EEQ/2016/000049.
